chunk_summary: Guidelines for outpatient SAM management in India, focusing on escalation criteria, defaulting risks, and discordant anthropometry decisions to ensure safe and effective care.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for escalating SAM cases from outpatient to inpatient care.
Identify strategies to reduce defaulting in outpatient CMAM programs in India.
Learn how to manage discordant anthropometry in SAM diagnosis and discharge decisions.
key_facts: Escalate to inpatient care if danger signs develop, appetite test fails, or oedema worsens.
High defaulting rates occur when travel is difficult or community referral is weak.
Frontline workers like ASHA reduce default risk through follow-up and case tracing.
MUAC and WHZ identify different risk groups; both should guide enrollment and discharge.
Do not discharge based solely on MUAC normalization if WHZ remains <-3, or vice versa.
reference_quote: Large Indian program experience has shown high defaulting when travel is difficult or when community referral is weak; referrals by frontline workers such as ASHA reduce default risk.
document_key_topics: Discordant anthropometry in SAM diagnosis and discharge
chunk_heading: SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION-(SAM) MANAGEMENT;OUTPATIENT CMAM  IMPLEMENTATION IN INDIA;OUTPATIENT ESCALATION DEFAULTING AND  DISCORDANT ANTHROPOMETRY DECISIONS;
